role_summary: Architect of the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom; longtime nonviolent-direct-action strategist; A. Philip Randolph's deputy; openly gay civil rights organizer in an era hostile to that visibility.
ai_reading: The corpus discusses Rustin across seven transcripts: Phil Hutchings (entry 98, SNCC chairman 1968), the Ladner sisters joint interview (entry 75), Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, Mississippi community-medicine), Doris Derby (entry 32), Lonnie C. King (entry 80, Atlanta sit-ins), and William S. Leventhal (entry 129). The cluster crosses SNCC, SCLC, and the Atlanta sit-in network, with the medical-organizing voice (Geiger) and the athlete-activist-network voice (Leventhal, close cosine to Russell and Carlos) as the surprising additions, suggesting Rustin's organizational vocabulary travels broadly across civil-rights cohorts the cultural reception treats as separate.
biographical_paragraph: Bayard Rustin is discussed in this corpus by 7 interviewees across the catalog (Phil Hutchings (entry 98), Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner) (entry 75), Dr. Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), H. Jack Geiger (partial read) (entry 54), Dr. Doris Derby (entry 32), Lonnie C. King (entry 80), William S. Leventhal (entry 129)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Bayard Rustin as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

sources.title: John D'Emilio, Lost Prophet: The Life and Times of Bayard Rustin
